message:
**2 replication attempts of this paper have been recorded in the FORRT Library of Reproduction and Replication Attempts (FLoRA).**


**Replication 1**

> Chen et al. (2022). A replication of “an experimental test of the expectancy‐disconfirmation theory of citizen satisfaction.” Public Administration, 100(3), 778–791. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/padm.12860 🔓 [Open Access](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdfdirect/10.1111/padm.12860)
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** successful
> **Quote:** We found support for the findings reported in Van Ryzin (2013) and concluded that the expectancy-disconfirmation model holds promise in a variety of settings as a framework for measuring citizen satisfaction with public services.
> **Quote source:** abstract


**Replication 2**

> Grimmelikhuijsen et al. (2017). Reconsidering the expectancy disconfirmation model. Three experimental replications. Public Management Review, 19(9), 1272–1292. https://doi.org/10.1080/14719037.2017.1282000 🔓 [Open Access](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/pdf/10.1080/14719037.2017.1282000?needAccess=true)
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** mixed
> **Quote:** Study 1 and 2 reproduced original findings. In contrast, study 3 indicates that expectation cues are retrospectively used to anchor prior experiences of performance.


Manual inspection may help to assess what led to the described outcome. Future research can learn from replications and build on them. More information about why and how we submitted this automated comment can be found [here](https://github.com/forrtproject/flora-pubpeer/blob/main/README.md)

> **Quote source:** abstract


**Replication 2**

> **Quote source:** abstract


**Replication 3**

> Rimol et al. (2026). Do 9-month-old infants prefer prosocial others? Two conceptual replications and a meta-analysis of the Hamlin & Wynn box opening/closing paradigm. Child Development. https://doi.org/10.1093/chidev/aacaf052 🔓 [Open Access](https://doi.org/10.1093/chidev/aacaf052)
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** failed
> **Quote:** In two independent samples of 9-month-olds, 58% of 24 Norwegian and 57% of 30 Javanese infants preferred the helper, which was not significantly different from chance.

> **Quote:** My key test was the odds ratio associated with the amount that subsidies change the choice towards a child care type, the first row in model 1, in the confirmatory analysis section above. In the authors original model, they found an odds ratio of 1.025, meaning that a unit increase in subsidies for the corresponding childcare alternative is associated with a 2.5% increase in the odds of preferring that mode of childcare. My experimental data produced a fairly similar result in terms of the key test. My computed odds ratio was 1.037, which is close in magnitude and the same level of significance, meaning the impact of subsidy on parent child care choice in my experiment was quite similar to the original. || My experimental data produced a fairly similar result in terms of the key test.
